metadata.title: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = photon-as-particle / quantum prediction / correct; CRIMSON = wave prediction / no shift / wrong
metadata.note: Exclusions: no relativistic-kinematics algebra, no Thomson scattering cross-section, no photoelectric effect recap, no Compton wavelength derivation.

beats.narration_text: Shine X-rays at a target and the scattered X-rays come back longer. They lost energy to the electrons they hit — exactly as if the photon were a billiard ball. In 1923 that killed any last hope that light was purely a wave.

props.headline: Shine X-rays at a target and the scattered X-rays come back…

card.copy: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons

beats.narration_text: Classical wave theory says this is impossible. A wave hitting an electron should shake the electron and re-emit radiation at the same frequency. The wavelength should not change. That prediction is clear and it is wrong.

props.headline: Classical wave theory says this is impossible. A wave hitting an…

beats.narration_text: Compton's 1923 measurement: X-rays at 0.071 nanometers scattered from graphite at 90 degrees come out at 0.073 nanometers. Two point four picometers longer. Repeatable. Classical physics gives zero shift.

props.headline: Compton's 1923 measurement: X-rays at 0.071 nanometers scattered from graphite at…

card.copy:
Incoming: 0.071 nm
Scattered at 90°: 0.073 nm
Classical prediction: no shift. Why the shift?

beats.narration_text: The answer: treat the photon as a particle carrying momentum p equals h over lambda. Now scatter it off the electron like a billiard-ball collision. The photon gives some of its energy to the recoiling electron — and less energy means longer wavelength.

props.headline: The answer: treat the photon as a particle carrying momentum p…

beats.narration_text: The shift depends only on the scattering angle. At 90 degrees, delta-lambda equals the Compton wavelength of the electron: 2.426 picometers. That constant is h over m-sub-e times c. It is a fundamental length scale set by the electron's mass.

props.headline: The shift depends only on the scattering angle. At 90 degrees,…

beats.narration_text: At 0 degrees — straight through — the shift is zero: the photon barely grazes the electron. At 180 degrees — bouncing straight back — the shift is double the Compton wavelength: 4.85 picometers. The formula captures every angle.

props.headline: At 0 degrees — straight through — the shift is zero:…

beats.narration_text: The energy transferred to the electron at 90 degrees is 0.24 kiloelectronvolts. That recoiling electron carries kinetic energy. This is not a thermal effect or an instrumental artifact — it is a direct measurement of light acting like a particle.

props.headline: The energy transferred to the electron at 90 degrees is 0.24…

beats.narration_text: Compton published in 1923. Classical physics had no answer. Wave theory could not produce a wavelength shift. The only model that matched was photon-as-particle with momentum. Compton received the Nobel Prize in 1927.

props.headline: Compton published in 1923. Classical physics had no answer. Wave theory…

beats.narration_text: The Compton effect sits alongside the photoelectric effect as proof that light carries discrete momentum. Together they make the same point: energy and momentum in a light beam come in packets. They are not continuous. The wave picture captures the distribution; the particle picture captures the collision.

props.headline: The Compton effect sits alongside the photoelectric effect as proof that…

beats.narration_text: X-rays change wavelength because photons carry momentum. The shift is Δλ = (h/mₑc)(1−cosθ). At 90° the shift is 2.4 pm. Classical waves cannot do this. Compton 1923.

props.headline: X-rays change wavelength because photons carry momentum. The shift is Δλ…

card.copy:
Photon momentum: p = h/λ.
Shift Δλ = (h/mₑc)(1−cosθ).
At 90°: Δλ = 2.4 pm (Compton 1923).

beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World series.
